tweets.text:
One of the main issues for China buying crude is the elevated freight cost. 

Now that a chunk of tankers that were stuck have now exited, it will be interesting to see what happens to tanker rates. 

At the moment, 10 tankers are exiting using the Iran route. Miracle Hope, which previously tried the Oman route just abruptly went north. 

Only time will tell what’s going on.

tweets.text:
Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours.

US tanker escort is back with a large group of tankers going out and coming in.

We tracked 3 VLCCs inbound ending with "prosperity". If you look at the VLCCs waiting in Fujairah, there will be another 3 to 4 Prosperity VLCCs going in.

The issue I'm seeing is that there aren't enough tankers to even do the ship-to-ship transfers at the speed needed to fully reverse the production shut-in.

Iran is also short on VLCCs, so it will use some of Iraq's loading to export its crude.

Flow rate is still insufficient, so while the oil market is contending with producers dumping barrels from floating storage and onshore storage, we need a much larger backlog to alleviate the real problem: production shut-in.

I have included the video from @MarineTraffic and included the table of inbound tankers.

Flow rate is still insufficient, so while the oil market is contending with producers dumping barrels from floating storage and onshore storage, we need a much larger backlog to alleviate the real problem: production shut-in.

tweets.text:
Here is something fascinating.

Gas Warich transited the Strait of Hormuz using the Oman lane. It is a sanctioned tanker. This will be the first time since the MOU that a sanctioned tanker used the Oman route. 

The inbound flow dropped off a cliff after July 3. Windward AI satellite showed no tanker dark transit on July 4.

We saw one big group escorted out, but nothing back in.

Very interesting.
tweets.full_text:
Here is something fascinating.

Gas Warich transited the Strait of Hormuz using the Oman lane. It is a sanctioned tanker. This will be the first time since the MOU that a sanctioned tanker used the Oman route. 

The inbound flow dropped off a cliff after July 3. Windward AI satellite showed no tanker dark transit on July 4.

We saw one big group escorted out, but nothing back in.

Very interesting.

tweets.text: The escorts are back! https://t.co/gL2bZngpHz
tweets.full_text: The escorts are back! https://t.co/gL2bZngpHz

tweets.text:
The last 24 hours in the Strait of Hormuz are a doozy.

At the start of the clip, you will see many of the vessels that were going through the Oman route take the Iran route instead.

Now there's something I should probably point out. In the last week or so, US escorts through the Oman lane typically saw VLCCs transit out; that was not the case this time. In addition, US transits typically show a cluster of ships moving together, which was not the case here either.

Basically, I'm just saying it doesn't look like a typical US escort. But it was also interesting that some of the vessels were able to leave right away via the Iran route, while others turned back.

I'm not entirely sure what's going on.

Visible AIS data-wise, there were no other escorts that took place. Inbound VLCCs were zero today for the first time since the MOU was signed. I will need to check the satellite data tomorrow, but I'm sure some went in; we just don't know right now.

What's clear, however, is that the Oman route will be heavily contested in the coming days. Any assumption of normality in the Strait of Hormuz is wishful thinking.

Here is the video from @MarineTraffic and I've included the table of inbound tankers.
tweets.full_text:

Now there's something I should probably point out. In the last week or so, US escorts through the Oman lane typically saw VLCCs transit out; that was not the case this time. In addition, US transits typically show a cluster of ships moving together, which was not the case here either.

Basically, I'm just saying it doesn't look like a typical US escort. But it was also interesting that some of the vessels were able to leave right away via the Iran route, while others turned back.

I'm not entirely sure what's going on.

Visible AIS data-wise, there were no other escorts that took place. Inbound VLCCs were zero today for the first time since the MOU was signed. I will need to check the satellite data tomorrow, but I'm sure some went in; we just don't know right now.

What's clear, however, is that the Oman route will be heavily contested in the coming days. Any assumption of normality in the Strait of Hormuz is wishful thinking.

Here is the video from @MarineTraffic and I've included the table of inbound tankers.
